summary: Gruber surveys the iPhone XR review consensus, finding unanimous agreement that it's the best dollar-for-dollar iPhone ever made. He highlights the two key reviewer disagreements: Panzarino misses the telephoto camera most while Patel considers the LCD display the bigger compromise. Gruber sides with Panzarino, saying he stopped noticing the display differences after a day but misses the telephoto daily. He also examines the industrial design compromise of a non-centered Lightning port, defending it as a necessary tradeoff for achieving a chin-free LCD display. The review concludes with a strong argument that $750 is not a 'crazy amount of money' for what is most people's primary computer, camera, and wallet, noting that inflation-adjusted iPhone prices haven't actually risen.
key_points: Every major reviewer reached the same bottom line: the iPhone XR is almost as good as the XS at a far lower price, making it the best value iPhone ever
The two main XR compromises — single camera vs. LCD display — matter differently to different people, but Gruber argues the missing telephoto is the bigger daily loss
Apple's engineering to make a chin-free LCD phone is unprecedented, and the off-center Lightning port is a worthwhile tradeoff almost no one will notice
When adjusted for inflation, iPhone XR pricing is essentially the same as the iPhone 4's launch price in 2010, undermining the narrative of ever-rising iPhone costs
Phones are no longer 'just phones' — they're primary computers, cameras, wallets, and more, making their prices reasonable compared to laptops and dedicated devices they replace
money_quotes: Dollar for dollar, the XR is almost certainly the best iPhone Apple has ever made.
After a day with the iPhone XR I stopped seeing anything wrong with the display or wider bezel. I miss the telephoto camera every day.
To my knowledge, iPhone XR is the only LCD phone ever made, by anyone, with no chin or forehead.
Everyone would notice if the XR had a chin; almost no one is going to notice the Lightning port is top-aligned rather than centered with the screws and speakers.
Phones are the most important computer in most people's lives. They're the only computer in many people's lives.
We've been corrupted by thinking of them as 'phones' in the pre-2007 sense of the word.
If you took an iPhone XR back to 2006 people would be amazed. If you told them they could buy one for $750 they'd think you were lying.
Considering how much more capable an iPhone XR is compared to an iPhone 4, I'd say $750 is an amazing bargain.
key_insight: The iPhone XR represents Apple's best value proposition ever, and the notion that modern phone prices are 'crazy' reflects outdated thinking about what phones actually are — they're our primary computers, cameras, and wallets, and inflation-adjusted, they cost about what they always have.
